result:
The air changes before the leaves do. There’s a subtle shift in the atmosphere, a crispness that sneaks in during the twilight hours when the sun dips below the horizon a little earlier than it did the week before. Summer doesn’t leave with a grand farewell; it slips out the back door while we are still sleeping with the windows open. 

I find this transition fascinating. The world holds its breath. The cicadas, which hummed so ferociously in August, begin to quiet their engines, their songs tapering off into a tired hum. The shadows stretch longer across the pavement, painting the afternoons in shades of gold and amber that feel borrowed from a fading dream. There is a melancholy in this shift, but it is a sweet kind of sadness. It is the feeling of turning the final page of a very good book—you are sorry it is ending, yet deeply satisfied by the journey.

It’s a time of quiet anticipation. We pull out thicker sweaters, not because we need them yet, but because we want to remember the weight of them, the comfort of wool against skin. We brew tea instead of iced coffee, not for warmth, but for the ritual of slowing down. Autumn is the season of the exhale. Summer is all inhalation—bright, loud, frantic, chasing the sun. But autumn invites us to pause, to let the smoke of our fleeting efforts curl into the cooling sky, and to find beauty in the art of letting go.
